1: Hebrew resumes (7:1). ʾaḥar hadəḇārîm hāʾēlleh — 'after these events/things.' The standard narrative transition formula signals a major chronological gap: Ezra 6 ends in 516 BCE; Ezra 7 begins in 458 BCE — a 57-year silence in the text. The narrative bridge is intentional: the Chronicler moves from temple completion (the first restoration) to Torah re-establishment (the second restoration under Ezra). bəmalkûṯ ʾArtaḥšaśtāʾ — Artaxerxes I (465–424 BCE).
2: Ezra's genealogy: ben-Śərāyāh ben-ʿAzaryāh ben-Ḥilqiyyāh — the ancestry traces from Ezra through Seraiah (the chief priest killed at Jerusalem's fall, 2 Kgs 25:18–21) backward toward Aaron. The genealogy is selective — 16 names for roughly 1,000 years from Aaron to Ezra — compressing generations as Matthew's genealogy does. The key link is the Zadokite/Aaronide lineage establishing Ezra's priestly credentials.
3: Continuation of the genealogical chain. ben-Zeraḥyāh ben-ʿUzzî ben-Buqqî — these names correspond to priests listed in 1 Chr 6:5–15. The Chronicler's genealogical data is consistent across books, suggesting use of temple archival records. The chain passes through Phinehas (v5), who received the covenant of perpetual priesthood (Num 25:13) — another anticipation of covenant faithfulness.
4: The genealogy continues through the priestly line. The listing of priests' ancestors serves the same function as royal genealogies: establishing legitimacy for office. Ezra's authority to teach Torah and lead reform rests on this Aaronide lineage — it is not merely personal merit but covenantal appointment by descent.
5: ben-Pîneḥās ben-ʾElʿāzār ben-ʾAhărôn hakkōhēn hārōʾš — 'son of Phinehas, son of Eleazar, son of Aaron the chief priest.' The genealogy terminates at Aaron, the first high priest. Phinehas received the covenant of a perpetual priesthood (Num 25:12–13) because of his zeal for YHWH's honor. Ezra inherits both the Aaronide office and, implicitly, the Phinehite zeal that will characterize his reform activities (Ezra 9–10).
6: sōpēr māhîr bəṯôrat Mōšeh — 'skilled scribe in the law of Moses.' Sōpēr (H5608) is the professional scribal class; māhîr (H4106) = swift, skilled, expert (cf. Ps 45:1 'my tongue is the pen of a sōpēr māhîr,' a skilled writer). Ezra's qualification is twofold: scribal craft (the ability to read, copy, and interpret texts) and the specific subject (the law of Moses). The combination defines the new post-exilic leader type: not king or prophet but Torah-scholar. yad-YHWH ʾĕlōhāyw ʿālāyw — 'the good hand of the LORD his God was upon him' — first occurrence of the recurring yad ṭôbāh formula (vv9,28; Neh 2:8,18).
7: ûmibənê yiśrāʾēl ûmin-hakkōhănîm — 'some of the Israelites, including priests, Levites, singers, gatekeepers, and Nethinim.' The Nethinim (nəṯînîm, H5411 = temple servants, 'given ones') were temple assistants, possibly descendants of non-Israelites who had joined the temple service (cf. Josh 9:27 Gibeonites). Their inclusion shows the breadth of the return: it was not only Judahites but the full temple-service community.
8: bah-ḥōdeš haḥămiššî — 'in the fifth month.' The journey from Babylon to Jerusalem: first month of departure (7:9), fifth month of arrival. The travel time (~4 months) matches the caravan distance (Babylon to Jerusalem ~900 miles). The dating to the 'seventh year of Artaxerxes' = 458 BCE if Artaxerxes I, or 398 BCE if Artaxerxes II (the date is debated; the 458 BCE reading is dominant).
9: kî bəʾeḥāḏ laḥōdeš hārîʾšôn — 'on the first day of the first month.' The departure on the first of Nisan is theologically charged: Nisan is the month of Passover (Exod 12:2 'this month shall be the beginning of months for you'). Ezra's departure begins on New Year's Day of the sacred calendar. yad ʾĕlōhāyw haṭṭôḇāh ʿālāyw — 'the good hand of his God upon him' — the formula (second occurrence) explains the successful journey: not skill but divine providence.
10: kî ʿEzrāʾ hēkîn liḇbô liḏrôš ʾeṯ-Tôrat YHWH wəlaʿăśôṯ wûllammeḏ bəyiśrāʾēl ḥōq ûmišpāṭ — 'For Ezra had devoted his heart to study the law of the LORD, to practice it, and to teach statute and ordinance in Israel.' This verse is the theological charter for the scribal vocation. The three verbs: dāraš (H1875 = seek/study), ʿāśāh (H6213 = do/practice), lāmaḏ (H3925 = teach). The order is not accidental: study precedes practice, practice precedes teaching. One cannot authentically teach what one has not first practiced; one cannot practice what one has not first studied. Hēkîn liḇbô — 'prepared/set his heart' — the hiphil of kûn + lēḇ: the heart as the organ of intentional commitment. The Talmud (b. Qiddushin 40b) quotes this verse as the paradigm for the proper order of learning and doing.
11: ništevan hakkiṯāḇ ʾăšer nātan hammelek — 'this is the copy of the letter that the king gave.' Ništevan (H5406) is a Persian loanword (nipishtāpana = dispatch/written order). Its occurrence here signals the switch back to Aramaic in vv12–26, where Artaxerxes' actual letter is embedded. The letter is described as 'the text' (nušḥāh) — an official copy for the record.
12: Aramaic section begins (7:12–26). Artaḥšaśtāʾ meleḵ malkayāʾ — 'Artaxerxes, king of kings.' The royal title meleḵ malkayāʾ (king of kings) is the standard Achaemenid superlative title, attested in Persian royal inscriptions (Darius: 'I am Darius the great king, king of kings'). The same title is applied to YHWH in 1 Tim 6:15 and to Christ in Rev 17:14; 19:16 — the NT appropriates the highest imperial title for the one true King. šəlām lāʿeṯ ûkəʿeṯ — 'peace in full, and now' — the Aramaic greeting formula before the substantive decree.
13: min-qoḏāmay śîm ṭəʿēm — 'I hereby decree' (lit. 'from before me a decree is placed'). The impersonal passive construction is typical of official Aramaic decrees — the decree takes on a semi-autonomous legal force, not just personal expression. kol-miṯnaddəḇ — 'whoever volunteers willingly' (Aramaic hitpael of ndḇ, H5068; cf. Hebrew nāḏaḇ, freewill offering). The departure is voluntary — the letter is not conscription but an invitation.
14: min-qoḏam malkāʾ — 'from before the king' — 'sent by the king and his seven counselors.' The seven counselors are historically attested: Esther 1:14 mentions the seven princes of Persia and Media; Herodotus (III.84) confirms a council of seven nobles in the Achaemenid court. kəḏāṯ ʾĕlāhāḵ — 'in accordance with the law of your God' — the decree explicitly validates Torah as the governing law within the Jewish community. The Persian crown delegates legal authority to divine law.
15: ûlhêṯāyāh kesef ûḏəhaḇ — 'to carry the silver and gold.' The freewill gifts recall the Exodus pattern (Exod 12:35–36, Israel 'spoiled' Egypt of silver and gold; Exod 36:3–7, freewill offerings for the tabernacle). The return from Babylonian exile reproduces the exodus-to-tabernacle movement: departure, gifts of precious metals, construction/dedication of a worship site. The vocabulary of mitnaddəḇ (v13) and nəḏābāh (freewill offering) recurs throughout the tabernacle account (Exod 25:2; 35:5,21,29).
16: kol-kesef ûḏəhaḇ ḏî-tiškaḥ — 'all the silver and gold you can gather/find.' The Aramaic šəkaḥ (H7912) = to find/gather; the same root in Ezra 5:17 (search the archives). The gathering of resources from the province of Babylon parallels the tabernacle-construction offering: voluntary contributions from the whole community. The parenthetical phrase 'freewill gifts of the people and priests' reinforces the voluntary, covenantal character of the giving.
17: bəʾosparnāʾ — 'diligently' (H629 = same adverb as 6:8,12,13). The word recurs to bind Artaxerxes' decree to Darius's — both kings require prompt, exact compliance. The animals listed for sacrifice (bulls, rams, lambs) mirror the Passover and dedication offering lists of ch. 6 — the restored community's sacrificial calendar is being re-established with imperial funding.
18: ûḏî-ʿălāḵ wəʿal-ʾaḥāyāḵ yîṭab — 'whatever you and your fellow countrymen decide is fitting.' The decree grants Ezra discretionary authority over the remainder of the silver and gold 'according to the will of your God' (kəreʿûṯ ʾĕlāhăḵôn). This is remarkable: the Persian king explicitly defers to divine will in the management of the funds. The Achaemenid policy of religious patronage reaches its maximum expression here.
19: wəʾănayyāʾ ḏî-miṯyəhiḇîn lāḵ — 'the vessels given to you.' The vessels are 'given' (yəhab, haphel passive) for the 'service of the house of your God' (pûlḥān bêt ʾĕlāhāḵ). Pûlḥān (H6399) = worship/service — the Aramaic term for temple liturgical service, cognate with Hebrew pālaḥ. The vessels are not property but sacred implements assigned to specific liturgical use.
20: min-bêt ginzê malkāʾ — 'from the royal treasury' (ginzê, H1596, from Persian ganj = treasury/storehouse). The treasury is available for any additional needs of the temple service. The phrase 'that you find occasion to supply' (ʾăšer yippal ʿălāyk, lit. 'that falls upon you') is an open-ended authorization — Ezra has blank-check authority for temple necessities, within reason.
21: ûminnenî ʾănaʾ Artaḥšaśtāʾ malkāʾ — 'I, King Artaxerxes, hereby order.' The first-person emphatic pronoun (ʾănaʾ) is unusual in imperial decrees — it personalizes the king's commitment. The treasurers of Trans-Euphrates are commanded directly, removing any bureaucratic intermediaries between Ezra's requests and royal fulfillment. The scope is deliberately large: all treasurers in the entire western satrapy must comply.
22: The enumerated limits — 100 talents of silver, 100 measures of wheat, 100 baths of wine, 100 baths of oil, and unlimited salt — are substantial but not unlimited. The 100-unit pattern may be a formulaic round number (a common administrative convention) rather than a precise budget. The addition 'and salt without limit' (ûmalaḥ ḏî-lāʾ kəṯāḇ) is notable: salt had covenant significance (cf. 4:14 'we eat the king's salt') and was essential for all sacrificial offerings (Lev 2:13).
23: kol-ḏî min-ṭəʿēm ʾĕlāh šəmayyāʾ — 'whatever the God of heaven commands.' The decree subordinates Persian imperial authority to YHWH's commands: 'carry it out faithfully for his house.' ûlmāh yihwēʾ qəṣap — 'for why should wrath come upon the kingdom?' The motivation is apotropaic: Persian support for the temple averts divine wrath from the empire. The theology is fully Ezra's (YHWH governs history), expressed in the pagan king's pragmatic terms. The same logic underlies Cyrus's decree (1:2–4) and Darius's (6:10).
24: wûlāḵôn məhôḏaʿîn — 'and we also declare.' The tax exemption for temple personnel — priests, Levites, singers, gatekeepers, Nethinim, and temple servants — is legally binding: 'it is not lawful (šallîṭ lāʾ) to impose tribute, tax, or duty on them.' This exemption protected the economic viability of the temple service; without it, the financial burden on temple workers would have made sustained temple service impossible.
25: wəʾantāh ʿEzrāʾ kiḥoḵmaṯ ʾĕlāhāḵ ḏî-beyadāḵ — 'And you, Ezra, using the wisdom of your God that you possess.' The delegation of judicial authority to Ezra is based on ḥoḵmaṯ ʾĕlāhāḵ — 'the wisdom of your God' — not just bureaucratic appointment. manne śāpəṭîn wəḏayyānîn — 'appoint judges and magistrates.' Ezra receives authority to establish courts for all Jews in Trans-Euphrates who know the Torah laws. The implicit standard: dāṯ ʾĕlāhāḵ (law of your God) and dāṯ malkāʾ (law of the king) are placed in explicit parallel — Torah and imperial law cooperate.
26: wəḵol-ḏî lāʾ lehěwēʾ ʿāḇēḏ dāṯ ʾĕlāhāḵ — 'anyone who refuses to obey the law of your God.' The penalties — death, exile, confiscation, imprisonment — are the standard four punishments of Achaemenid criminal law (attested in Persian legal texts). Notably, violating God's law and the king's law carry the same legal consequences. The imperial decree enforces Torah compliance with the full weight of Persian penal authority. This is the furthest extent of the 'law of God and law of king' convergence in the Achaemenid period.
27: Bārûḵ YHWH ʾĕlōhê ʾăḇōṯênû — 'Blessed be the LORD, the God of our ancestors.' The Hebrew resumes; Ezra breaks into spontaneous doxology. This is one of the rare first-person passages in Ezra (9:1–15 is the other), shifting from third-person narrative to Ezra's own voice. The blessing formula (bārûḵ) is the standard Hebrew doxology (cf. Gen 24:27; 1 Sam 25:32; 2 Sam 18:28; Ps 28:6; 31:21). ʾăšer nātan kāzōʾt bəlēb hammelek — 'who put such a thing as this in the king's heart' — nātan bəlēb (put in heart) is the idiom for divine inspiration of human decision (Neh 2:12; 7:5; Prov 21:1). YHWH's sovereignty over the Persian king's heart is the theological climax of the chapter.
28: wəʿālay hiṭṭāh ḥeseḏ — 'He has shown me steadfast love' (ḥeseḏ, H2617 = covenant faithfulness, steadfast love). Ḥeseḏ is the covenant-loyalty word par excellence — it encompasses faithfulness, mercy, and sustained commitment. That Ezra attributes the king's favor to YHWH's ḥeseḏ toward him personally is both personal testimony and theological interpretation: the imperial decree is YHWH's covenantal provision. waʾăḥazzēq ḵəyad YHWH ʾĕlōhay ʿālay — 'I strengthened myself as the good hand of the LORD my God was upon me' — the third occurrence of the yad formula (vv6,9,28), framing the entire chapter with divine enablement. Ezra's strength is derivative; the hand of YHWH is the true agent.
